identified_error: The study used independent t-tests for statistical comparisons involving four groups measured at multiple time points, instead of appropriate methods for multiple group comparisons (e.g., ANOVA/repeated measures ANOVA) with correction for multiple comparisons. Independent t-tests between pairs of groups without adjustment for multiplicity massively inflate the Type I error rate, producing spuriously significant results.
why_fatal: Using independent t-tests for pairwise comparisons among four groups across four time points generates a large number of comparisons without controlling the family-wise error rate. This dramatically increases the probability of false-positive findings. The reported significant differences between treatment and placebo groups, and between different dose groups, cannot be trusted because the statistical method was inappropriate. The entire pattern of 'significant' results for anthropometric and biochemical outcomes — which form the basis of all the paper's conclusions about ACV efficacy, dose-dependence, and time-dependence — may be artifacts of multiple testing inflation rather than true treatment effects.
correct_approach: The appropriate statistical approach for a four-group, repeated-measures design (measurements at weeks 0, 4, 8, and 12) would be a mixed-model repeated measures ANOVA (or linear mixed-effects model) with group as the between-subjects factor and time as the within-subjects factor, testing for group × time interactions. Post-hoc pairwise comparisons should then be conducted only if the omnibus test is significant, with appropriate corrections for multiple comparisons (e.g., Bonferroni, Tukey, or similar adjustments). This would properly control the Type I error rate across all comparisons.
additional_errors: Baseline fasting blood sugar levels (113-120 mg/dL) are in the prediabetic/diabetic range, which is unusual for supposedly healthy adolescents/young adults with no chronic diseases — raising questions about data integrity or inclusion criteria adherence.
Convenience sampling was used rather than proper probability sampling, limiting generalizability.
No power analysis or sample size justification was provided.
The placebo (lactic acid solution) may not be truly inert — lactic acid itself could have metabolic effects, potentially biasing comparisons.
No mention of intention-to-treat analysis, dropouts, or adherence verification beyond phone reminders.
Diet diary and physical activity were reportedly recorded but no quantitative data or formal analysis comparing groups was presented.
The study included minors as young as 12 years old, yet BMI thresholds used (27-34 kg/m²) are adult obesity criteria rather than age- and sex-specific pediatric percentile-based criteria.
No trial registration number is mentioned, raising concerns about prospective registration.
